verses.text: And it came to pass after this, that Absalom the son of David had a fair sister, whose name was Tamar; and Amnon the son of David loved her. And Amnon was so vexed, that he fell sick for his sister Tamar; for she was a virgin; and Amnon thought it hard for him to do any thing to her. Guidance: 'After this' — the narrator places the chapter immediately after Nathan's word in chapter 12. The sword has not departed from David's house, and we are about to see its first cut. The cast is named carefully. Absalom (David's third son by Maacah of Geshur, per 3:3) had a full sister Tamar — both children of the same mother. Amnon (David's firstborn by Ahinoam, 3:2) was Tamar's half-brother. The narrator's phrasing — 'a fair sister' / 'Amnon… loved her' — sets up the perversion: he names her as Absalom's sister first, then says Amnon 'loved' her. The Hebrew verb ahav can describe genuine love, but in this chapter it will turn out to be obsession, then violence, then hate. Amnon is so fixated he becomes physically sick. 'For she was a virgin; and Amnon thought it hard for him to do any thing to her' — Tamar lived in the royal women's quarters, was protected, and a sexual relationship with her (legally his half-sister, which Lev 18:9, 20:17 explicitly forbids) was both incest and impossible to arrange through normal social means. The chapter is showing us the moral physics of obsession: lust that cannot be approached by legitimate means produces sickness, then conspiracy, then assault. The chapter is the most painful in 2 Samuel; the narrator handles it with restraint, but does not spare us the moral diagnosis.

And it came to pass after this, that Absalom the son of David had a fair sister, whose name was Tamar; and Amnon the son of David loved her. And Amnon was so vexed, that he fell sick for his sister Tamar; for she was a virgin; and Amnon thought it hard for him to do any thing to her. Guidance: 'After this' — the narrator places the chapter immediately after Nathan's word in chapter 12. The sword has not departed from David's house, and we are about to see its first cut. The cast is named carefully. Absalom (David's third son by Maacah of Geshur, per 3:3) had a full sister Tamar — both children of the same mother. Amnon (David's firstborn by Ahinoam, 3:2) was Tamar's half-brother. But Amnon had a friend, whose name was Jonadab, the son of Shimeah David's brother: and Jonadab was a very subtil man. And he said unto him, Why art thou, being the king's son, lean from day to day? wilt thou not tell me? And Amnon said unto him, I love Tamar, my brother Absalom's sister. And Jonadab said unto him, Lay thee down on thy bed, and make thyself sick: and when thy father cometh to see thee, say unto him, I pray thee, let my sister Tamar come, and give me meat, and dress the meat in my sight, that I may see it, and eat it at her hand. Guidance: Enter Jonadab. He is Amnon's cousin (son of David's brother Shimeah, also called Shammah, 1 Sam 16:9) and 'a very subtle man.' The Hebrew chakam me'od is normally translated 'very wise,' but used here it is the chapter's quiet sarcasm — 'wisdom' used for evil ends becomes cleverness without conscience. Jonadab is the friend who, instead of restraining Amnon, designs the scheme. He notices Amnon is getting thinner ('lean from day to day') and probes for the cause. Amnon confesses: 'I love Tamar.' Jonadab does not say 'this is wrong.' He says 'here's how to arrange to be alone with her.' The plan is precise: fake illness, ask the king specifically for Tamar to bring food to your bedroom, eat from her hand. Every detail is calculated to produce private access. The chapter is showing us how sin is amplified by the wrong counselor. Many of Amnon's worst hours pass with Jonadab nearby; a cousin who would not enable might have saved Tamar. There is a category of friendship that fails by collaborating with a friend's worst desire instead of opposing it. Jonadab is the type, and the chapter will return to him in v32 with a small redemption — his information will at least clarify the casualty report after the murder.

So Amnon lay down, and made himself sick: and when the king was come to see him, Amnon said unto the king, I pray thee, let Tamar my sister come, and make me a couple of cakes in my sight, that I may eat at her hand. And David sent home to Tamar, saying, Go now to thy brother Amnon's house, and dress him meat. Guidance: The trap is set. Amnon performs sickness; David visits; Amnon makes the request. The wording is innocent on the surface — just a sick brother asking for the food of his half-sister, baked in his own quarters. But the specifics give it away to anyone alert: why must Tamar specifically come? Why must she bake in his sight rather than send the food prepared? David does not catch the strangeness. He sends instructions to Tamar to go. The chapter is showing us a David who has lost the moral sharpness he had earlier. He should have asked why; he should have sensed something off. The Bathsheba moment has, the narrator implies, made David less able to read his own household. Sin in a leader's life does not just produce personal consequences; it dulls the leader's ability to detect sin around him. David, the father, sends his daughter into the trap his unrepentant son set.

And David sent home to Tamar, saying, Go now to thy brother Amnon's house, and dress him meat. Guidance: The trap is set. Amnon performs sickness; David visits; Amnon makes the request. But the specifics give it away to anyone alert: why must Tamar specifically come? Why must she bake in his sight rather than send the food prepared? David does not catch the strangeness. He sends instructions to Tamar to go. The chapter is showing us a David who has lost the moral sharpness he had earlier. He should have asked why; he should have sensed something off. The Bathsheba moment has, the narrator implies, made David less able to read his own household. David, the father, sends his daughter into the trap his unrepentant son set.

So Tamar went to her brother Amnon's house; and he was laid down. And she took flour, and kneaded it, and made cakes in his sight, and did bake the cakes. And she took a pan, and poured them out before him; but he refused to eat. And Amnon said, Have out all men from me. And they went out every man from him. Guidance: Tamar comes obediently, kneads flour, bakes the cakes in front of Amnon as he watches from his bed, presents the food. Amnon refuses to eat. Then he orders 'have out all men from me' — clear the room. The household servants leave. The chapter is letting us watch the trap close one stage at a time. Each of Tamar's actions has been innocent; each of Amnon's has been calibrated. The room empties. She is alone with the half-brother who has been planning this. The narrator's careful pacing makes the reader want to shout the warning none of the chapter's characters know to shout. Sometimes the most painful pages of scripture are the ones where the victim, doing everything right, walks into a danger no one she trusted has flagged for her.

And she took a pan, and poured them out before him; but he refused to eat. And Amnon said, Have out all men from me. And they went out every man from him. And Amnon said unto Tamar, Bring the meat into the chamber, that I may eat of thine hand. And Tamar took the cakes which she had made, and brought them into the chamber to Amnon her brother. And when she had brought them unto him to eat, he took hold of her, and said unto her, Come lie with me, my sister. Guidance: Amnon escalates. 'Bring the meat into the chamber' — i.e., into his private inner room, away from any household traffic. Tamar still suspects nothing; she has been told her brother is ill and needs food brought close to him. She enters the inner room. 'He took hold of her, and said unto her, Come lie with me, my sister.' The hand on her arm; the demand. The chapter has reached the moment Tamar did not see coming. The form of address — 'my sister' — is one of the most chilling phrases in the Old Testament; he names the relationship he is about to violate as he proposes the violation. The verbal recognition of the incestuous category does not stop him; it just hangs over the scene. The chapter is showing us a victim who has done nothing wrong, in a private room, suddenly aware that the half-brother she came to nurse means her great harm. What Tamar does next will be the chapter's clearest moral voice.

And she answered him, Nay, my brother, do not force me; for no such thing ought to be done in Israel: do not thou this folly. And I, whither shall I cause my shame to go? and as for thee, thou shalt be as one of the fools in Israel. Now therefore, I pray thee, speak unto the king; for he will not withhold me from thee. Guidance: Tamar's plea is one of the most morally articulate speeches in the Old Testament. Four arguments, perfectly laid out under crisis pressure. (1) 'Nay, my brother, do not force me.' Direct refusal, addressing him as brother to invoke family obligation. The verb anah means 'humiliate, do violence to' — Tamar names the act precisely. (2) 'No such thing ought to be done in Israel.' She invokes the covenant community's moral consensus; this kind of act is not permitted among the LORD's people. (3) 'Do not thou this folly… thou shalt be as one of the fools in Israel.' The word 'folly' (nevalah) is the technical Hebrew term for an outrageous moral act, the same word used in Gen 34:7 of the rape of Dinah. Tamar names exactly what category this is, and names the social consequence — Amnon will be one of the 'fools' (nevalim) of Israel, a man whose reputation cannot recover. (4) 'I, whither shall I cause my shame to go?' She names her own future — the violated unmarried princess has nowhere to take her shame; her life is ruined. (5) Then she offers an alternative: 'speak unto the king; for he will not withhold me from thee.' This last is the most generous of all her arguments. She suggests Amnon ask David for permission to marry her properly — a problematic suggestion since Leviticus 18 forbids the marriage, but in the patriarchal narratives (Abraham/Sarah; cf. Gen 20:12) half-siblings had married, and Tamar is reaching for any face-saving alternative. She is willing to be married to her assailant rather than be raped. The chapter is showing us a woman whose moral clarity under terror is breathtaking. She refuses, names the act, names the social consequence, offers an exit, and pleads. Amnon hears all of it. He chooses anyway.

Howbeit he would not hearken unto her voice: but, being stronger than she, forced her, and lay with her. Then Amnon hated her exceedingly; so that the hatred wherewith he hated her was greater than the love wherewith he had loved her. And Amnon said unto her, Arise, be gone. Guidance: The narrator records the act in one Hebrew clause — 'being stronger than she, forced her, and lay with her' — and does not linger. Sexual violence is named, not described. The chapter respects the victim by refusing to make the moment a spectacle. What the chapter does linger on is the aftermath. 'Then Amnon hated her exceedingly; so that the hatred wherewith he hated her was greater than the love wherewith he had loved her.' The Hebrew is emphatic with comparison: the new hate is greater than the old 'love.' The reversal is the chapter's deepest moral diagnosis. What Amnon called 'love' was never love. It was lust dressed as love; it disappeared the instant the lust was satisfied, and what remained was loathing. The chapter is teaching us the moral physics of lust: it consumes its object, and once consumed it despises what it consumed. True love would have refused the assault; lust has used the assaulted person and now has nothing to do with her. Amnon's first word to Tamar after the rape is 'arise, be gone.' Even the food she made, even the body he just violated, even her presence — all rejected. The chapter is showing us the bottomless cruelty of someone who has used another human being.

And she said unto him, There is no cause: this evil in sending me away is greater than the other that thou didst unto me. But he would not hearken unto her. Then he called his servant that ministered unto him, and said, Put now this woman out from me, and bolt the door after her. Guidance: Tamar speaks again. 'There is no cause: this evil in sending me away is greater than the other that thou didst unto me.' The legal context is critical. Deuteronomy 22:28-29 required a man who had violated an unbetrothed virgin to marry her and never divorce her — the only legal cover for the destroyed marriage prospects of the woman. By sending Tamar away unmarried, Amnon is denying her even the inadequate Torah remedy. The second wrong (banishment) is, in Tamar's analysis, worse than the first (rape) — because the second condemns her to a lifetime of unmarriageable disgrace. The Hebrew is precise; she is reasoning legally even from the trauma. Amnon does not listen. He calls the servant: 'Put now this woman out from me, and bolt the door after her.' He cannot even say her name; she is 'this woman' now. The verbal demotion from 'my sister' (v11) to 'this woman' (v17) is the chapter's record of the inner change in Amnon. Bolting the door is the final humiliation — the act not just of rejection but of exclusion. Tamar is removed and the door is locked behind her. The chapter is showing us the complete picture of how a perpetrator treats a victim he no longer has use for. Every detail is calibrated to maximum cruelty.

And she had a garment of divers colours upon her: for with such robes were the king's daughters that were virgins apparelled. Then his servant brought her out, and bolted the door after her. And Tamar put ashes on her head, and rent her garment of divers colours that was on her, and laid her hand on her head, and went on crying. Guidance: The narrator pauses on Tamar's clothing. The 'garment of divers colours' (ketonet passim) is the same phrase used of Joseph's coat in Gen 37:3 — the long, ornamental, sleeved tunic that marked special status. Here it is the official uniform of unmarried royal princesses. Tamar walked into Amnon's house wearing the badge of her virginity; she walks out having been violated. Then her response, every gesture a public sign of grief. (1) Ashes on her head — the standard Israelite mourning gesture. (2) Tearing the ornamented robe — destroying the very garment that signified the now-lost virginity. (3) Hand on her head — the gesture of overwhelming sorrow. (4) 'Went on crying' — Hebrew halakhah ve-za'aqah, a continual loud cry that travels through the streets. Tamar refuses to hide what has happened. She walks home in full mourning, audibly weeping, with her princess-robe in pieces. The chapter is showing us a victim who does not stay silent — and the public mourning becomes the only justice available to her in the chapter, because no one else will move. She is making the rape public the only way she can. There is a profound moral dignity in her refusal to muffle herself. The chapter is also showing us that Tamar's voice is loud enough to reach her family, even when the legal system will fail.

And Absalom her brother said unto her, Hath Amnon thy brother been with thee? but hold now thy peace, my sister: he is thy brother; regard not this thing. So Tamar remained desolate in her brother Absalom's house. And when king David heard of all these things, he was very wroth. But Absalom spake unto his brother Amnon neither good nor bad: for Absalom hated Amnon, because he had forced his sister Tamar. Guidance: Three responses in three verses. (1) Absalom: 'Hath Amnon thy brother been with thee?' He knows immediately, just from the robe and ashes. The euphemism 'been with thee' is the only thing he can say in front of household servants. Then he silences her: 'hold now thy peace, my sister; he is thy brother; regard not this thing.' The Hebrew is gentler than the English — Absalom is not telling her to pretend it did not happen; he is telling her to keep it quiet for now while he plans the response. But the practical effect on Tamar is silencing. She moves into Absalom's house — 'desolate' (shomemah, devastated, ruined) — and disappears from the narrative. Her life is over. (2) David: 'when king David heard… he was very wroth.' He is angry. He does not act. Levitical law required execution for incest (Lev 20:17); David, as judge of Israel, should have ordered it. He does not. The narrator does not tell us why — possibly because David, knowing his own Bathsheba sin, cannot bring himself to condemn another for sexual misconduct; possibly because Amnon is the firstborn and presumptive heir; possibly simple paralysis. The chapter is showing us a father whose moral authority over his sons is now compromised by his own past. His anger is real; his action is absent. (3) Absalom: 'spake unto his brother Amnon neither good nor bad: for Absalom hated Amnon.' Silent rage. Absalom does not confront, does not denounce, does not forgive — he files the hatred and waits. Two years is going to pass before he moves. The chapter is showing us three failures of response: Tamar silenced, David paralyzed, Absalom storing vengeance. None of the three responses includes justice for Tamar. She remains desolate. The chapter is one of the most painful in the Old Testament because the victim is so completely abandoned by everyone who should have stood for her.

Then he silences her: 'hold now thy peace, my sister; he is thy brother; regard not this thing.' The Hebrew is gentler than the English — Absalom is not telling her to pretend it did not happen; he is telling her to keep it quiet for now while he plans the response. But the practical effect on Tamar is silencing. She moves into Absalom's house — 'desolate' (shomemah, devastated, ruined) — and disappears from the narrative. Her life is over. (2) David: 'when king David heard… he was very wroth.' He is angry. He does not act. Levitical law required execution for incest (Lev 20:17); David, as judge of Israel, should have ordered it. He does not. The narrator does not tell us why — possibly because David, knowing his own Bathsheba sin, cannot bring himself to condemn another for sexual misconduct; possibly because Amnon is the firstborn and presumptive heir; possibly simple paralysis. The chapter is showing us a father whose moral authority over his sons is now compromised by his own past. His anger is real; his action is absent. (3) Absalom: 'spake unto his brother Amnon neither good nor bad: for Absalom hated Amnon.' Silent rage. Absalom does not confront, does not denounce, does not forgive — he files the hatred and waits. Two years is going to pass before he moves. The chapter is showing us three failures of response: Tamar silenced, David paralyzed, Absalom storing vengeance. None of the three responses includes justice for Tamar. She remains desolate. The chapter is one of the most painful in the Old Testament because the victim is so completely abandoned by everyone who should have stood for her.

And Absalom her brother said unto her, Hath Amnon thy brother been with thee? but hold now thy peace, my sister: he is thy brother; regard not this thing. So Tamar remained desolate in her brother Absalom's house. And when king David heard of all these things, he was very wroth. But Absalom spake unto his brother Amnon neither good nor bad: for Absalom hated Amnon, because he had forced his sister Tamar. Guidance: Three responses in three verses. (1) Absalom: 'Hath Amnon thy brother been with thee?' He knows immediately, just from the robe and ashes. The euphemism 'been with thee' is the only thing he can say in front of household servants. Then he silences her: 'hold now thy peace, my sister; he is thy brother; regard not this thing.' The Hebrew is gentler than the English — Absalom is not telling her to pretend it did not happen; he is telling her to keep it quiet for now while he plans the response. But the practical effect on Tamar is silencing. She moves into Absalom's house — 'desolate' (shomemah, devastated, ruined) — and disappears from the narrative. Her life is over. (2) David: 'when king David heard… he was very wroth.' He is angry. He does not act. Levitical law required execution for incest (Lev 20:17); David, as judge of Israel, should have ordered it. He does not. The narrator does not tell us why — possibly because David, knowing his own Bathsheba sin, cannot bring himself to condemn another for sexual misconduct; possibly because Amnon is the firstborn and presumptive heir; possibly simple paralysis. The chapter is showing us a father whose moral authority over his sons is now compromised by his own past. His anger is real; his action is absent. (3) Absalom: 'spake unto his brother Amnon neither good nor bad: for Absalom hated Amnon.' Silent rage. Absalom does not confront, does not denounce, does not forgive — he files the hatred and waits. Two years is going to pass before he moves. The chapter is showing us three failures of response: Tamar silenced, David paralyzed, Absalom storing vengeance. None of the three responses includes justice for Tamar. She remains desolate. The chapter is one of the most painful in the Old Testament because the victim is so completely abandoned by everyone who should have stood for her.

And it came to pass after two full years, that Absalom had sheepshearers in Baal-hazor, which is beside Ephraim: and Absalom invited all the king's sons. And Absalom came to the king, and said, Behold now, thy servant hath sheepshearers; let the king, I beseech thee, and his servants go with thy servant. Guidance: 'After two full years.' Two years of nursing his hatred, waiting for the perfect occasion. The chapter is showing us how long it can take a wound to ferment into revenge in a heart that does not surrender it. Sheep-shearing — the same festival background as the Nabal episode of 1 Sam 25 — produced the most relaxed public gatherings of the year. Baal-hazor is in northern Judah, about fourteen miles north of Jerusalem, near the Ephraim border. Absalom invites 'all the king's sons.' Multiple invitations, broad attendance — the perfect cover. Then he goes to David and invites the king himself, plus all the royal servants. The detail tells us that Absalom has timed and planned this for a long time.

And it came to pass after two full years, that Absalom had sheepshearers in Baal-hazor, which is beside Ephraim: and Absalom invited all the king's sons. And Absalom came to the king, and said, Behold now, thy servant hath sheepshearers; let the king, I beseech thee, and his servants go with thy servant. Guidance: 'After two full years.' Two years of nursing his hatred, waiting for the perfect occasion. The chapter is showing us how long it can take a wound to ferment into revenge in a heart that does not surrender it. Sheep-shearing — the same festival background as the Nabal episode of 1 Sam 25 — produced the most relaxed public gatherings of the year. Baal-hazor is in northern Judah, about fourteen miles north of Jerusalem, near the Ephraim border. Absalom invites 'all the king's sons.' Multiple invitations, broad attendance — the perfect cover. Then he goes to David and invites the king himself, plus all the royal servants. The detail tells us that Absalom has timed and planned this for a long time.

And the king said to Absalom, Nay, my son, let us not all now go, lest we be chargeable unto thee. And he pressed him: howbeit he would not go, but blessed him. Then said Absalom, If not, I pray thee, let my brother Amnon go with us. And the king said unto him, Why should he go with thee? But he pressed him, that he let Amnon and all the king's sons go with him. Guidance: David declines on grounds of cost — bringing the whole royal entourage to a sheep-shearing would be too expensive for Absalom. Absalom presses but accepts the refusal with a blessing. Then he makes the real request: 'let my brother Amnon go with us.' David asks the right question: 'Why should he go with thee?' The question shows that David is alert to something off — why specifically Amnon, given the silent enmity? But David does not press further. Absalom 'pressed him,' and David relents. Amnon and 'all the king's sons' are sent with Absalom. The chapter is showing us a David who has the right instincts (the suspicion in the 'why?' question) but who lacks the will to follow through. He had asked the right question two years ago about Amnon and Tamar; he should have asked harder now. The chapter is recording the slow erosion of a king's once-sharp judgment.

Now Absalom had commanded his servants, saying, Mark ye now when Amnon's heart is merry with wine, and when I say unto you, Smite Amnon; then kill him, fear not: have not I commanded you? be courageous, and be valiant. And the servants of Absalom did unto Amnon as Absalom had commanded. Then all the king's sons arose, and every man gat him up upon his mule, and fled. Guidance: The plan. Absalom's servants will wait for Amnon to be drunk, then attack on Absalom's signal. The drunkenness is calculated — Amnon will be slow to react. Absalom's 'fear not… have not I commanded you?' reassures the servants that the responsibility (and legal consequence) for the killing rests on him, not them; they are following orders. 'Be courageous, and be valiant' echoes the language Joshua and Solomon hear from God before great tasks (Josh 1:6-9; 1 Kgs 2:2); Absalom is profaning the holy battlefield exhortation for an assassination. The kill happens. Amnon — David's firstborn — is dead. 'Then all the king's sons arose, and every man gat him up upon his mule, and fled.' Royal princes rode mules; the panic-flight on mules is the standard biblical image of high-rank emergency departure. The half-brothers, terrified, ride for their lives back toward Jerusalem. The first 'fold' of the fourfold restitution David pronounced in chapter 12 has now been collected. Amnon is gone. The sword that David let off the leash in his unprosecuted son has come back into his house.

Now Absalom had commanded his servants, saying, Mark ye now when Amnon's heart is merry with wine, and when I say unto you, Smite Amnon; then kill him, fear not: have not I commanded you? be courageous, and be valiant. And it came to pass, while they were in the way, that tidings came to David, saying, Absalom hath slain all the king's sons, and there is not one of them left. Then the king arose, and tare his garments, and lay on the earth; and all his servants stood by with their clothes rent. Guidance: The first report reaches David in the worst possible form. 'Absalom hath slain all the king's sons, and there is not one of them left.' The rumor has been amplified by the panic; the messenger saw the king's sons fleeing en masse from a scene of violence and concluded that they were the only survivors, that the rest were dead. The number escalates in the telling — from 'one dead' to 'all dead.' David hears this and collapses. He tears his clothes, lies on the ground — the same posture he had during the dying-child fast in chapter 12. The royal servants tear their clothes in solidarity. The palace believes it has lost every son. The chapter is showing us how quickly bad news, in conditions of panic and partial information, can become worse news. The next verses will partially correct the report — but the corrected version is still catastrophic.

And it came to pass, while they were in the way, that tidings came to David, saying, Absalom hath slain all the king's sons, and there is not one of them left. Then the king arose, and tare his garments, and lay on the earth; and all his servants stood by with their clothes rent. Guidance: The first report reaches David in the worst possible form. The next verses will partially correct the report — but the corrected version is still catastrophic.

And Jonadab, the son of Shimeah David's brother, answered and said, Let not my lord suppose that they have slain all the young men the king's sons; for Amnon only is dead: for by the appointment of Absalom this hath been determined from the day that he forced his sister Tamar. Now therefore let not my lord the king take the thing to his heart, to think that all the king's sons are dead: for Amnon only is dead. Guidance: Jonadab — the same Jonadab who designed the rape scheme — now provides the truth that calms David's panic. 'Let not my lord suppose that they have slain all the young men the king's sons; for Amnon only is dead.' The narrator's irony is intricate: the man who enabled the original crime now also explains the consequence two years later. 'By the appointment of Absalom this hath been determined from the day that he forced his sister Tamar.' Jonadab knew Absalom was planning this revenge. He has been watching it unfold for two years. The chapter is revealing what the court already understood — the silence between Amnon and Absalom (v22) was readable as future violence by everyone with eyes to see. David did not see. The chapter is gently exposing how much the king did not know about what was happening under his own roof. Jonadab's information ends in 'Amnon only is dead' — partial relief from the panic, but the relief is itself the confirmation of fratricide. David's heir is dead by the hand of his third son. The corrected report does not soften the catastrophe.

The corrected report does not soften the catastrophe.

But Absalom fled. And the young man that kept the watch lifted up his eyes, and looked, and, behold, there came much people by the way of the hill side behind him. And Jonadab said unto the king, Behold, the king's sons come: as thy servant said, so it is. And it came to pass, as soon as he had made an end of speaking, that, behold, the king's sons came, and lifted up their voice and wept: and the king also and all his servants wept very sore. Guidance: 'But Absalom fled.' Three words. The murderer is gone, north toward his maternal grandfather's kingdom at Geshur (v37 will confirm). The royal sons arrive at Jerusalem — alive, except for Amnon — and the city sees the catastrophe in real time. The watchman spots the dust-cloud of incoming mules; the king's sons appear; everyone breaks down in tears. David's wail is matched by the wail of every servant present. The royal household, which had just been in panic-mourning over the rumored death of all the sons, now collapses into actual mourning over the confirmed death of Amnon and the disappearance of Absalom. The chapter is showing us a family in grief, weeping together for the second time in two chapters. The sword has cut deep. The first half of David's fourfold loss has now been collected: the baby in chapter 12, Amnon in chapter 13. Two more sons (Absalom in chapter 18, Adonijah in 1 Kings 2) will follow over the coming decades.

But Absalom fled, and went to Talmai, the son of Ammihud, king of Geshur. And David mourned for his son every day. So Absalom fled, and went to Geshur, and was there three years. And the soul of king David longed to go forth unto Absalom: for he was comforted concerning Amnon, seeing he was dead. Guidance: Absalom takes refuge with his maternal grandfather Talmai, king of Geshur — a small Aramean kingdom east of the Jordan, north of Israel. Geshur is outside David's jurisdiction; Absalom is politically safe there. He will stay three years. Meanwhile, two things in David: 'David mourned for his son every day' — for Amnon, the dead heir, daily grief. Then: 'the soul of king David longed to go forth unto Absalom: for he was comforted concerning Amnon, seeing he was dead.' Over time, the grief for Amnon eased — there is, after all, a finite duration to mourning even the dead — and was replaced by longing for the absent Absalom. The Hebrew construction tells us that David's heart did two contrary things at once. He had lost two sons in different ways: one to death, one to exile. The death-grief subsided; the exile-grief grew. Chapter 14 will narrate the long process by which Joab eventually engineers Absalom's return. The chapter we have just read ends with the family in three pieces: a dead firstborn (Amnon), an exiled murderer-son (Absalom), and a desolate violated daughter (Tamar) — and a king who is somehow expected to govern Israel through the rest of it. The sword has cut, and is still cutting, and David carries the wound back into the next chapters of state business. The chapter is the precise fulfillment of Nathan's word: the sword has not departed from David's house, and the evil that 'I will raise up against thee out of thine own house' is now visibly the work of one son against another. The kingdom continues; the king grieves; the chapter ends without closure. Tomorrow, chapter 14, will begin the work of partial reconciliation that will, paradoxically, set up Absalom's full rebellion in chapter 15. The family's wounds are not going to heal cleanly. The chapter has shown us why.
